item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed with this opinion, but I upvoted anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea itself may work, but the execution is lacking, the logic is iffy and the "There's something intelligent behind this dangerous thing! sPo0oO0kY!!1" has been overused to death (The fact that it's written as a throwaway thing, with no real exploration of the concept, makes it worse). I would recommend a rewrite of the rewrite that excised the half baked "intelligent" menace behind this thing, the whole deal with the revenant D class, the D class containment breach and the "It will devour the whole universe in a couple hundred years if we don't stop it!!" thing, for good measure. You may also want to rethink the acquisition log because it also doesn't make any sense.

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The execution here is rather poor. Let's go.

First off, if the thing consumes any matter save for silver or copper, this means it also consumes nitrogen, oxygen and whatever other gases the atmosphere consists of. This means it acts like a hole into deep space, sucking air into it with extreme force.
This also means that agent Wilson shouldn't have been able to open an airtight chest with vacuum inside. And these 6 D-Class shouldn't have been able to open the vacuum chamber. Some people aren't able to twist the lid off a jar, and we're talking deeper vacuum and a much larger space here.
The way it moves is really odd. It doesn't obey gravity, but it accounts for the rotation and movement of the Earth, the Solar system and the galaxy.
Which also means that if left in deep space, this thing is pretty much safe. It will consume stray hydrogen atoms and the occasional meteor, but will otherwise stay put. It's going to take a looooooong time expanding to the size of an asteroid. And will probably have a huge copper layer around it by then, preventing other matter from entering.

beyond the confides

Should probably say "confines" here.

extended his right hand into SCP-2435-1, which was consumed up to the wrist

Does 2435-1 have a wrist?

with the amount of copper atoms in the bodies

Did they count individual atoms?

next thing we knew SCP-2435-2 was found inside D-19098

How did the guy's innards not get sucked into 2435-2?

irregular ripples on the surface of both instances

How did they notice that, given the objects are immaterial and essentially invisible?

created a vacuum effect at their point of impact

As I said earlier, individual objects should suck air pretty good on their own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, based on the fact that no one has mentioned it, maybe I am just crazy. But isn't there a Series I article about a large black hole that does the exact same thing, but releases ambient noise and can't be contained? Also, grows constantly, not just with "food"?

Maybe I'm crazy. I feel like if I wasn't crazy, someone would've mentioned it.

EDIT: Oh yeah, by the way, I liked it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fine, but my point still stands. 1.7*10-8 meter or 1.7E-8

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an English language site, we use periods as the decimal point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One easy fix to boost it looking more real:

SCP-2435-1 expands as it consumes matter, gaining 0.00000017 meters on all sides for every 1 gram it consumes.

No-one in science can be bothered to write it out like that, so using scientific notation (in this case, 1,7*10-8 meter or 1,7E-8 meter in shorthand)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, did I at least get a new hand afterward?

Either way, very interesting and well written. Also, any chance this was inspired by Reboot?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Are these things see-through?

Anyway, they're really cool, especially the You Lose part. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've changed

"SCP-2435-2 is incapable of consuming silver, which have the same properties on it as copper does on SCP-2435-1."

to

"SCP-2435-2 is incapable of consuming silver, which has the same effect on it as copper does on SCP-2435-1."

If this is wrong, or overstepping the bounds of permitted editing, change it back.

item.title: Re: Possible Error
item.content:encoded: Whoops, there's always more tiny little errors. Different drafts were bigger you see, so let me go ahead and change that little problem! Thanks for spotting that!

item.title: Possible Error
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed the article for the most part, and any complaints I have were already said by others. However, I'm a bit confused that in the Containment Procedures, it says to see Incident G for what happens when -1 & -2 come in contact. However, the Incident G log doesn't actually exist, and the two come in contact in Incident D.

+1, btw

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one got rewritten sufficiently well! I'm still not fully on-board with it yet (moreso that I'm not into the idea and it's a bit too dense for me to really wrap my head around), but downvote removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright. So I've had a chance to look this over since your big edit. You've removed enough things that were getting to me that I'm going to upvote (though I still think this would be vastly improved by the removal of the black boxes and their replacement via the actual information). I like the idea of this object as a weapon, and the possibility that their are more (maybe one for all the metallic elements) sitting around somewhere.

+1 Well done.

Also, I noticed you had one instance of copper molecules still in the article, so I changed it to atoms.

item.title: Re: Nice one
item.content:encoded: Aha, that's what I thought.

item.title: Re: Nice one
item.content:encoded: SCP-2435-2 is a lot smaller currently than its big brother. They can only take in material up to their surface area, meaning the second cube has to take in smaller amounts before hitting the same amount as number one. It'll catch up but right now it's too small to do so.

item.title: Nice one
item.content:encoded:
I liked it. An original object with enough story behind it to get me to upvote.

One slight question: Why is SCP-2435-2 theorized to take so much longer to consume the universe compared to SCP-2435-1? (The number of digits for #2 is bigger than #1).
Any specific reason or is it just random?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops! Thanks for pointing that out, I meant to take out the word "molecules" but I guess those two slipped through!

item.title: M8 I like it too
item.content:encoded: It's nice and creepy. But next time, split the paragraphs

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
consume copper molecules

consuming silver molecules

Are you meaning atoms? These metals are both chemical elements.

item.title: a liitle too much black boxes
item.content:encoded: Although it creates a feeling of the unknown, if you put too much black boxes, it'll get annoying.

item.title: Revisions
item.content:encoded:
First off, thank you to everybody who provided info about what they didn't like, or what seemed unnecessary to them! It helped greatly with how to smooth out the rough edges on this so far!

As to what I've done to update, I went ahead and removed -3 entirely from the article. It did seem tacked on upon thinking of it further. In its place I added in a few more tiny clues as to their origin/what these are. I believe the new sentences are in tone and have proper grammar but if there's minor issues please let me know.

I kept some of the black box redactions, but did remove all but two minor Redacted/Data expunged's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall, the SCP is pretty interesting and a little bit creepy too, but there were a few points where I had to stop reading in confusion. Namely, I had to go back a few times to check which SCP was which, and by the end I wasn't entirely sure why you chose copper and silver as the elements that aren't consumed.

As for the universe consumption, as far as I know, we don't know how large the universe is. Predictably infinite. So to say it will consume all of the universe in a number of years doesn't sound like a scientific hypothesis, especially considering it would be a lot more helpful to know how fast this is on a planetary scale. Maybe say it would consume the Earth in a number of months, or the known universe in a number of years?

As a final note, I second the above comments about redaction. The redactions should be toned down a little bit, especially if others are complaining about it too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, Sapes!

I worked on this with you in draft form, but yeah, I didn't give you enough on the second go-around. However, yes, there's enough that it's now intriguing, but let's see if we can make it even better. Since you're the author, you can modify this as you see fit without problem.

As everyone else has said, the stuff with -1 and -2 is great. It takes something that looks like an impartial physics anomaly, and turns it into a threat with an apparent intelligence behind it. That's great. You've done a good job with that, and any tightening should keep that narrative.

As for -3, you've got two options: either tell us more about it, or remove it entirely. Honestly, either way works, because the narrative around -1 and -2 is strong enough on its own.

As for the redactions, removing names and dates are fine. The greatest problems are around -3, which means either talk more or remove it altogether (the more I think about it, the more removal sounds like the better option). Then there's this sentence:

Agent ██████ [DATA EXPUNGED].

That's no good. It doesn't work because either they're annihilated by the space (in which case, we know it does that, no need to expunge it) or they only dips their hand in it or whatever (in which case, we have no clue that they did it, and the expungement harms comprehension). I'd say spell this out, and then think about how that affects everything else. For example, if the agent is sucked into the space, presumably the agent has been deposited somewhere, but perhaps the Foundation doesn't know where.

In any case, the votes indicate you're definitely on to a good SCP. Cleanup is all it needs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. The core concept is solid, but the use of censoring is boring - the third addendum could be pretty much anything, for instance. With some editing to tighten it up, I'm fairly confident this would warrant an update.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I am rather torn on this one.

On one hand, I dig the effects of -1 and -2. They are simple, yet creepy, and remind me of some old school SCP articles.

On the other hand, you really hit a lot of my personal peeves in this with all the redaction and black boxing you do. -3 might not as well be mentioned, since we know so little about it other than the fact that it may or may not be spooky that including it at all seems like an excuse to use the phrase [REDACTED]. You also go overboard with the black boxing here. Why not include the names of the agents, doctors, containment site, dates of the incident? There doesn't really seem to be a whole lot gained by the narrative by censoring that information. Again, you just seem to be censoring information for the sake of censoring information.

I'm going to no vote this for now. I really dig -1 and -2. But man, this article has some issues to sort out before it gets close to +1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dug it quite a bit myself. +1!

late edit: I do agree that the over-redaction of -3 is annoying, but the stuff with -1 and -2 was interesting and unique enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the idea but feel it could be tightened up a little. Also I'm not a fan of -3, since we really don't hear anything about it. I think it should be cut or explained- perhaps it's expanding far slower than the others but is currently uncontainable? Whatever the deal is with it, you give us too little to be intriguing.

Tentative +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The tone and grammar are fine, and I don't mind the idea. However, I think the containment procedures read a little awkwardly for me. It's not that they're extremely outlandish by any means, but I think everything being sealed within a seal sounds a bit repetitive to read. However, that's a pretty petty complaint on my end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I don't know what the issues with this are, maybe I'm in a charitable mood, but this one was sufficiently weird and creepy to get me onboard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's an unpopular opinion, but I actually liked this one. It's well written for the most part, and the object itself is pretty interesting. I don't know, maybe I'm just a minority here.
